note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-08-01-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM, numpy rfft): the Σ(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 201.858s but the ffprobed master is 201.404s (0.45s off, past the ±0.3s gate). Cross-correlation shows the master is the 14 blocks concatenated with an ~0.4s gap after each; per-file ffprobe durations are inflated ~0.03s each by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, and the error accumulates (diff reaches -0.469s by s14), which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset (multiples of the 0.08s mp3-frame grid); final end = ffprobed master duration 201.404s.

phrases.text: Open the Operations module, then Issues.
This is Maintenance Issues.
Every equipment problem across your sites lands here as one work order.
A dryer that won't heat, a touch screen that quit, a leaking pump.
One list tracks them all the way to fixed.
Start at the top.
Four numbers frame the board: how many issues are open, how many are being worked,
how many are high priority, and the average time to a fix.
Before you read a single row, you know how heavy the load is.
First panel, the twelve-month trend.
It counts issues reported over the last year and flags the recent change.
A rising line means more tickets coming in.
Useful when someone asks whether last month was really busier, or it just felt that way.
Next, the priority breakdown.
Low, Medium, High, and Critical, each as a share of the total.
This is your triage picture.
When High and Critical creep up, that's where the day's attention goes.
Third panel, the pipeline.
It splits the open work into Resolved, in Progress, and Blocked.
Blocked is the one to watch.
A blocked job is stuck waiting on a part or a person, and it won't clear itself.
Now the list itself.
The header calls it the Issue List and gives the record count.
The subtitle says the job out loud: work order tracking.
Everything above this card is just the summary.
Top-right of that card, two buttons.
New Issue opens a blank work order.
Export pulls the list out to a file.
We won't export anything today; just know where it is when a manager asks for it.
Click New Issue to see the form.
This is where a technician writes up a new problem: what broke, where, and how urgent.
We're only looking, so we back out without saving.
Nothing gets logged.
Below the buttons, the search row.
Type a few letters to find an issue by title,
or open Filters to narrow by status, priority, or site.
On a long list, this is how you reach one ticket fast.
The table has seven columns.
Which site logged it, the title, its status, its priority,
who owns it, who reported it, and the date.
Read across one row and you have the whole story of a work order.
Look at the live examples.
An air dryer not reaching temperature, marked Open and high.
A conveyor belt squeaking in the tunnel, already In Progress.
A chemical pump leak, Critical, and already Resolved.
Real problems, each at a different stage.
Notice two columns are blank.
Assigned To and Created By show a dash on every row here.
In a live tenant they carry names.
A row with no owner is your cue that somebody still needs to pick it up.
At the foot of the list, the counter.
It reads five of five, on one page.
When your network fills this up, the arrows here walk you through the rest.
So that's the work-order board.
The four numbers up top tell you the load, the three panels tell you its shape,
and the list is where every fix gets tracked.
New Issue starts one; the columns tell you where it stands.
This is the page you live in for anything broken on site.
